---
title: "GenAI in Pharma"
subtitle: "From personal experiment to the one-person department — and the guardrails that keep it honest"
author: "Peng Zhang"
date: 2026-06-05
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default]
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    history: true
    transition: slide
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    footer: "GenAI in Pharma 2026 — Virtual"
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#0b3d5c"
---

## Where this talk comes from

::: {.incremental}
- I started using AI as a **personal productivity experiment** — not a strategy.
- Then I saw **Eric's slides**. They reframed the question for me:
  the issue isn't *whether* AI helps, it's **knowing what Claude can actually do**.
- Once you know the ceiling, you start using it in **very different ways**.
:::

. . .

> This talk is the path from *"a tool I poke at"* to *"how a regulated pharma org should think about it."*

---

# Part 1 — What changes {background-color="#0b3d5c"}

## Many ways to use Claude

Not one workflow — a spectrum:

::: {.incremental}
- **Ask / chat** — quick reasoning, drafting, explaining
- **In the IDE** — Claude in **RStudio**, Claude in **VS Code**
- **Claude Code** — agentic work against a real repo
- **Agentic + automated workflows** — chained, repeatable, hands-off
:::

. . .

The mode you pick changes *who does the work* — and *who checks it*.

## The one-person department

::: {.incremental}
- AI doesn't just speed people up — it **collapses whole workflows** onto one person.
- Work that needed a team (or a vendor, or a ticket-and-wait) is suddenly **affordable to each individual**.
- "AI will replace people" is the scary framing.
  The real shift: **the one-person department becomes possible.**
:::

## Affordable — almost embarrassingly so

::: {.incremental}
- Cost per workflow has dropped to the point where I can **build things on my phone** with Claude Code.
- The barrier is no longer **budget** or **headcount**.
- The barrier moves somewhere new — to **us**.
:::

---

# Part 2 — ...but not everyone, and not everything {background-color="#0b3d5c"}

## People become the bottleneck

::: {.incremental}
- An agent learns a new domain **faster than a person can**.
- So the limiting factor stops being the tool — it becomes **human understanding**.
- To some extent, **what a human can comprehend and verify** is now the real ceiling.
:::

. . .

That's uncomfortable, but it's the honest version of "AI replaces people."

## Human-in-the-loop — a counter-example

::: {.incremental}
- "Keep a human in the loop" sounds like a safety guarantee.
- But if the human **can't actually evaluate the output** fast enough or deeply enough,
  the loop is **theater**, not control.
- A rubber-stamp reviewer is *worse* than honest automation — it hides the gap.
:::

. . .

So the question becomes: **what, at minimum, must a human review** for an AI-generated outcome?

## Pre-defined requests don't disappear

::: {.incremental}
- Not everything should be an open-ended prompt.
- **Shiny apps and packages are still needed** — for repeatable, validated, pre-defined requests.
- Agents are great at the novel and the messy;
  **fixed tools are still better for the known and the regulated.**
:::

---

# Part 3 — Doing this in Pharma {background-color="#7b1d3a"}

## Governance is the hard part

In a regulated environment, capability is the easy half. The other half:

::: {.incremental}
- How do we **validate and evaluate a "skill"** before we trust it? → a **working group**
- **Data privacy** — HIPAA, GDPR
- **Confidential information & trade secrets** — what can even leave the building?
- **Where does the model run?** — trust in **Anthropic vs Azure vs Amazon Bedrock**
:::

## GxP on validated platforms

A GenAI workflow that touches regulated work still owes the usual:

::: {.incremental}
- **Access control**
- **Traceability**
- **Audit trails**
- **Encryption**
- Separate **production and development** environments
:::

. . .

"It's just a chatbot" is not a validation strategy.

## Tools, validated and connected

::: {.incremental}
- **Validated tools + functions** the agent is allowed to call
- A **knowledge base** the work is grounded in — mine lives in **Obsidian**
- **Data models** *(Doug's point)* — agents are only as good as the structure underneath them
- Domain pipelines like **pycsr** — purpose-built, auditable steps
:::

---

# Part 4 — Where this is heading {background-color="#0b3d5c"}

## When the model gets good enough

::: {.incremental}
- Imagine the LLM becomes powerful enough to map **accurately onto the data model**.
- The friction we design around today — parsing, reconciling, reformatting — **shrinks**.
- What's left is the part that was always hard: **deciding what's correct.**
:::

## Who is the document *for*?

::: {.callout-note title="A question I didn't expect to ask"}
Is this document built for a **human to read** — or for an **agent to read**?
:::

::: {.incremental}
- The answer changes how we **write, structure, and store** everything.
- Human-readable and agent-readable are **starting to diverge** — and we'll have to choose, or serve both deliberately.
:::

## Three modes of human oversight

::: {.incremental}
- **Human-in-the-loop** — human approves each step
- **Human-on-the-loop** — human supervises, intervenes on exception
- **Human-in-command** — human sets intent and bounds; system runs within them
:::

. . .

Different work needs different modes — pick deliberately, don't default.

## Accountability doesn't move

::: {.incremental}
- The model can do the work. It **cannot hold the responsibility**.
- **People still hold the accountability** — for the output, the decision, the patient impact.
- That's not a limitation to engineer away. It's the **point**.
:::

---

# Takeaways {background-color="#0b3d5c"}

## What I'd leave you with

::: {.incremental}
1. The **one-person department** is real and already affordable.
2. The new bottleneck is **human understanding**, not cost or compute.
3. In pharma, **governance, GxP, and data privacy** are the work, not the footnote.
4. Decide your **oversight mode** on purpose.
5. **Accountability stays with people** — design for that, don't pretend otherwise.
:::

## Things I'm still chewing on

- A **Claude Code course** — getting the team to the same baseline
- Documents authored **for agents**, not just humans
- How to make "human review" **mean something** under real time pressure

. . .

Thank you — questions welcome.
